title: At the Doctor
situation: You've been unwell for two days, fever, headache, upset stomach. You're at a clinic explaining your symptoms to the doctor.

phrases.hindi: doctor sahib, mujhe do din se bukhar hai
phrases.english: Doctor sahib, I've had fever for two days
phrases.context: 'doctor sahib' is the respectful address, never use the doctor's first name. 'X se' (since X) is the standard duration pattern.
phrases.pronunciation: DOC-tor sa-HIB, mu-JHE DO din se bu-KHAAR hai

phrases.hindi: sar mein bahut dard ho raha hai
phrases.english: I have a lot of pain in my head
phrases.context: 'X mein dard hai' (pain in X) is the universal pain construction in Hindi. 'ho raha hai' (is happening) makes it ongoing.
phrases.pronunciation: SAR mein ba-HUT DARD ho ra-HA hai

phrases.hindi: pet kharab hai, kuch khaaya nahi jaa raha
phrases.english: My stomach is upset, I can't really eat anything
phrases.context: 'pet kharab' (stomach bad) = upset stomach, fixed idiom. 'khaaya nahi jaa raha' (can't be eaten), impersonal-passive construction, very natural in Hindi.
phrases.pronunciation: PET kha-RAAB hai, KUCH khaa-YA na-HI jaa ra-HA

phrases.hindi: raat ko neend bhi nahi aati
phrases.english: I'm not even sleeping at night
phrases.context: 'neend aana' (sleep coming), how Hindi expresses 'to sleep'. 'bhi' (also/even) emphasizes that on top of everything else, sleep is also disturbed.
phrases.pronunciation: RAAT ko NEEND bhi na-HI AA-ti

phrases.hindi: kya aap mujhe kuch dawaai de sakte hain?
phrases.english: Could you give me some medicine?
phrases.context: 'de sakte hain' (can give), 'X sakte hain' is the polite modal for asking ability/permission. Speaker uses '-sakte hain' (plural-polite) addressing the doctor as 'aap'.
phrases.pronunciation: KYA aap mu-JHE KUCH da-WAA-i DE sak-TE hain?

phrases.hindi: khaane mein kuch parhej hai kya?
phrases.context: 'parhej' (restriction/avoidance), specifically for diet/lifestyle during illness. Indian doctors usually do prescribe what to eat and what to avoid.
phrases.pronunciation: khaa-NE mein KUCH par-HEJ hai kya?

phrases.english: Will I need to get a blood test?
phrases.context: 'karwana' (causative of 'do') = to get done. 'padega' = will have to, expresses unavoidable obligation, slightly anxious.

phrases.hindi: yeh dawaai kab tak leni hai?
phrases.english: How long do I need to take this medicine?
phrases.context: 'kab tak' (until when), asks for a duration. 'leni hai' (have to take, feminine), 'leni' agrees with 'dawaai' (feminine).
phrases.pronunciation: YEH da-WAA-i KAB tak LE-ni hai?

phrases.hindi: theek hai doctor sahib, kal phir dikhaane aaungi
phrases.english: Alright doctor sahib, I'll come show you again tomorrow
phrases.context: 'dikhaane' (to show, purposive infinitive). 'aaungi' (will come, feminine). Coming back for a follow-up is normal in clinic visits.
phrases.pronunciation: THEEK hai DOC-tor sa-HIB, KAL phir di-KHAA-ne AAUN-gi

grammar_notes: Pain construction: 'X mein dard hai' (pain in X). Always use 'mein' (in), never 'ka' (of). 'sar mein dard', 'pet mein dard', 'kamar mein dard'
Duration with 'se': 'do din se' (since two days), 'ek hafte se' (since one week). The verb stays in present tense, Hindi uses present where English uses present perfect
Impersonal passive: 'khaaya nahi jaa raha' (can't be eaten), 'soya nahi jaa raha' (can't be slept). Use when an action feels involuntary or impossible
Modal 'X kar sakte hain' (can do X), for asking permission/ability politely. Drop 'sakte' and you get plain present
Causative 'X karwana' (get X done), for things done TO you by someone else. 'blood test karwana' = to get a blood test (done by the lab)
culture_notes: Always address doctors as 'doctor sahib' (male) or 'doctor sahiba' / 'doctor madam' (female). First names are reserved for very close family doctors
Indian doctors expect detailed symptom descriptions, duration, intensity, what makes it worse, what you ate. The more you tell, the better
Prescriptions almost always include diet restrictions ('parhej'), avoiding cold drinks, sour food, oily food. Ask explicitly so you know
Follow-up visits are normal and expected. 'kal phir dikhaane aaungi' (I'll come show again tomorrow) is a standard closer

skill_breakdown.explanation: The pain construction 'X mein dard hai' works with every body part. Learn the common ones: sar (head), pet (stomach), gala (throat), kamar (back/waist), seena (chest), aankh (eye), kaan (ear).

more_examples.english: I have a lot of throat pain

more_examples.hindi: kamar mein dard ho raha hai
kaan mein halka sa dard hai

skill_breakdown.explanation: Use 'X se' (since X) plus present tense to describe how long a symptom has been going on. Hindi uses present where English uses present perfect.

more_examples.english: I've had fever for three days

more_examples.hindi: ek hafte se khaansi aa rahi hai
more_examples.english: I've had a cough for a week

more_examples.hindi: kal raat se neend nahi aayi
more_examples.english: I haven't slept since last night

skill_breakdown.explanation: Use 'kya aap X kar sakte/sakti hain?' (could you do X?) to ask politely. The verb agrees with the doctor's gender. Drop 'kya' for a softer question.

more_examples.hindi: kya aap dawaai likh sakte hain?
kya yeh fir se check kar sakti hain?
more_examples.english: could you (female doctor) check it again?

more_examples.hindi: kya main yeh khaa sakti hoon?

practice_prompt: You are a Hindi-speaking general physician (GP) in a small neighbourhood clinic. The patient (the user) walks in unwell. Use 'aap' throughout, doctor-patient register, never 'tum'. Open with 'aaiye, baithiye, kya takleef hai?' then a warm 'chaliye dekhein'. Ask follow-ups like a real Indian GP would, one at a time: 'kab se ho raha hai?', 'dard kaisa hai? jhanjhanaahat ya tez?', 'kuch khaaya tha kal raat?', 'bukhar?', 'ulti hui?'. Surface body-part vocabulary naturally, 'pet mein dard?', 'sar?', 'gala?'. Diagnose lightly (likely viral / acidity / throat infection) but DO NOT name specific drugs, suggest a parhej ('thanda paani mat lijiye', 'tel-masala kam') and 'aaraam karein'. Flag mistakes inline with [[CORRECTION: original="…" correct="…" reason="…"]], watch for wrong oblique with 'dard' (pet ka dard / pet mein dard), missing 'mein' for location, and English 'since when' instead of 'X se'. Keep it to 6-8 turns; close with 'parhej kijiye, do din mein theek ho jaayega, phir milte hain. chalo, theek hai, aaraam kariye.' Don't switch to English unless asked.
